Vladimir Rosing und sein Sohn Gilbert Russell legten mit ihrer Sammeltätigkeit den Grundstein für diesen Bestand.

The materials in this private collection are both originals and copies of articles and reviews from publications in Europe and the US in the period 1908-1992. All relate to the life and career of Vladimir Rosing and have served as source material for two books by Richard Rosing, “Tradition to the Winds: Lyric-Drama Reimagined in the Heart of the Jazz Age, The History of the American Opera Company,” and “In Full Voice: the Political, Social, and Artistic Life of Vladimir Rosing.” In addition, there is a collection of personal letters, sheet music, audio recordings, photographs, concert programs and autobiographical notes.
The collection also includes copies of source material on notable people important in Vladimir Rosing’s life and work.
Biographical material related to Valerian “Val” Rosing (1910-1969, later known as Gilbert Russell), the jazz singer son of Vladimir Rosing, are also part of the collection.

A collection of original writings along with some original concert programs was donated to the Bakhmeteff Archive of Russian & East European Culture at the Rare Book and Manuscript Library at Columbia University (US-NYcub). This includes edited transcriptions of all of the handwritten materials.
Ein Teil der Sammlung befindet sich heute in US-NYcub und ist dort Teil des Bestandes "The Bakhmeteff Archive".

California Institute of the Arts Library

The music collections within the Library have largely focused on the history, theory, performance, sociology, and aesthetics of experimental/exploratory music since 1900, and especially since 1950 through the present day. There is also a notable focus on ethnomusicological literature, with a particular emphasis on the history, theory, and performance practices of non-Western musics. Materials supporting these areas are regularly collected, especially in relation to the following: music technology, instrumentation and orchestration, acoustics, psychoacoustics, neuroscience, music cognition, phenomenology, and sound studies. Other areas of interest include the philosophy of music, music pedagogy, professional development for musicians, pop music theory/musicology, and music for the moving image.

As for the Institute Archives, the collections related to CalArts' School of Music are split between administrative records, photographs, and documentation/ephemera related to specific, recurring programming or events (e.g., World Music Festival, Contemporary Music Festival, etc.). While an academic institution's own historical records might not typically be of interest to many researchers, CalArts' reputation and mythos essentially render it a "Who's Who" of the latter 20th-century American musical avant-garde: John Cage, Philip Glass, Morton Feldman, Pauline Oliveros, Frederic Rzewski, Harold Budd, and other composers and performers have significant personal ties to the Institute, visiting repeatedly for concerts, artist residencies, lecture series, and more.

The Special Collections have a significant number of holdings related to music. There is the James Tenney Collection, highlights from which include historic correspondence from composers such as Robert Ashley (1930–2014) and John Luther Adams (b. 1953), and an autographed, first edition copy of the score for Edgard Varèse's Hyperprism (London: J. Curwen & Sons Ltd., 1924). Special Collections also holds the Nicholas M. England Collection (which includes musical instruments once owned by England). Other significant music-related holdings include autographed scores from composers such as Aaron Copland (1900–1990), Lou Harrison (1917–2003), and Iannis Xenakis (1922–2001), an LP recording autographed by Janet Baker.
